model_narrative: This is a refinery planning problem. We buy crude oils and may import some components, run the refinery units at chosen process activity levels to distill and crack the crude into intermediate streams, and blend those streams into finished products that are then sold to meet demand. We decide how much of each crude to buy, how much of each component to import, how hard to run each process, how to move material through the transfer options, how to pool component streams into the quality-blended products, and which recipe blends to run for the recipe-blended products. The objective is to make the operating profit as large as possible.

sets.name: c
sets.doc: all commodities that move through the refinery, including crude oils, intermediate streams produced by the units, components that may be imported, and the final products

sets.name: cr
sets.doc: the crude oils that can be purchased and fed to the refinery

sets.name: ci
sets.doc: the commodities that can be bought in from outside as imported components

sets.name: cf
sets.doc: the final products that are sold

sets.name: cfq
sets.doc: the final products that are made by quality blending, where individual component streams are pooled and the blend must meet quality specifications

sets.name: cfr
sets.doc: the final products that are made by recipe blending, where fixed recipes set the proportions of component streams

sets.name: k
sets.doc: the productive process units of the refinery

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the processes the units can run, each consuming some commodities and producing others

sets.name: kuse
sets.doc: the pairs of a unit and a process that say which process runs on which unit

sets.name: bposs
sets.doc: the allowed pairs of a quality-blended final product and a component stream that may go into it; the first entry is the product and the second is the eligible blending stream

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the quality specifications a product can be measured against, such as vapor pressure, octane number, percent boiled at 212F, sulfur, and viscosity blending number

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the two ways a product quantity is measured, by volume or by weight

sets.name: ms
sets.doc: the pairs telling which measure basis each specification is expressed on; the first entry is the measure and the second is the specification. Volume-based specifications blend on a volume basis and weight-based specifications blend on a weight basis

sets.name: cfm
sets.doc: the pairs of a final product and a measure that the model is required to track for that product; the first entry is the product and the second is the measure

sets.name: l
sets.doc: the two kinds of quality limit, a lower limit and an upper limit

sets.name: r
sets.doc: the alternative blending recipes available for the recipe-blended products

sets.name: tr
sets.doc: the transfer options that move material between streams

params.name: m3tob
params.doc: barrels per cubic meter

params.name: pbmg
params.doc: lead content allowed in motor gas, in grams per liter

params.name: ppb
params.doc: price of lead, in dollars per kilogram

params.name: ocpb
params.doc: lead-related operating cost, in dollars per cubic meter of gasoline

params.name: crdat
params.doc: crude oil data table keyed by crude and attribute; attributes include supply (availability), price, transport (transport cost per ton), and gravity

params.name: ddat
params.doc: final product data table keyed by product and attribute; attributes include price (selling price per ton) and demand

params.name: kdat
params.doc: unit capacity data table keyed by unit and attribute

params.name: ap
params.doc: process yield of a commodity by a process, in 1000 tons of commodity per unit of process activity; a negative value means the commodity is consumed by the process and a positive value means it is produced

params.name: recipes
params.doc: recipe blending coefficient giving how much of a component stream goes into a recipe-blended product under a given recipe

params.name: at
params.doc: transfer coefficient of a commodity in a transfer option; a negative value means the commodity is consumed by the transfer and a positive value means it is produced

params.name: specs
params.doc: the quality limit value for a product at a lower or upper limit on a specification; a value of zero means that limit does not apply to that product and specification

params.name: prop
params.doc: physical property of a commodity for a given specification, such as its octane or sulfur level

params.name: char
params.doc: characterization factor converting a quantity of a commodity into a given measure basis; weight is one and volume reflects the commodity density

params.name: bp
params.doc: capacity utilization of a unit by a process, in cubic meters of capacity used per ton of process activity

params.name: kp
params.doc: available capacity of a unit, in 1000 cubic meters

params.name: oc
params.doc: operating cost of a unit, in dollars per cubic meter of capacity used

params.name: pcr
params.doc: plant-gate price of a crude oil, in dollars per ton

params.name: pimp
params.doc: price of an imported component, in dollars per ton

params.name: invent
params.doc: net inventory change of a commodity, in 1000 tons

params.name: dir
params.doc: the sign convention for a limit, minus one for a lower limit and plus one for an upper limit, used so the over-or-under slack pushes the blend in the right direction

vars.name: u
vars.doc: amount of crude purchased, in 1000 tons

vars.name: z
vars.doc: activity level of each process, in 1000 tons

vars.name: cap
vars.doc: capacity used at each unit, in 1000 cubic meters

vars.name: trans
vars.doc: level of each transfer activity, in 1000 tons

vars.name: import_var
vars.doc: amount of each component imported, in 1000 tons

vars.name: bq
vars.doc: amount of a component stream sent into a quality-blended product, in 1000 tons

vars.name: rb
vars.doc: level of a recipe blend run for a product, in 1000 tons

vars.name: q
vars.doc: quantity of a final product expressed on a given measure basis, in 1000 tons or 1000 cubic meters

vars.name: ov
vars.doc: amount by which a blend is over or under a quality limit, in 1000 units

vars.name: sales
vars.doc: amount of a final product sold, in 1000 tons; fixed to product demand

vars.name: revenue
vars.doc: total revenue from selling final products, in 1000 dollars

vars.name: recurrent
vars.doc: total recurrent operating cost, in 1000 dollars

vars.name: purchase
vars.doc: total cost of purchasing crude and imported components, in 1000 dollars

vars.name: transport
vars.doc: total transport cost of crude, in 1000 dollars

vars.name: profit
vars.doc: operating profit, in 1000 dollars

constraints.name: u[c]
constraints.intent: For every commodity, the refinery must stay in material balance. Everything that makes the commodity available has to equal everything that uses it up. On the supply side a commodity becomes available when it is a purchased crude, when the processes produce it, when the transfer activities yield it, and when it is brought in as an imported component. On the using side it is consumed as it is pooled into the quality-blended products, as it is drawn down or built up by the planned inventory change, and as it is consumed by the recipe blends of the recipe-blended products. Balance these two sides exactly for each commodity, remembering that the process and transfer yields already carry their own sign so that consumption shows up as a negative contribution.

constraints.name: cap[k]
constraints.intent: For each process unit, the capacity it uses must equal the capacity drawn by all the processes that run on it. Add up, over every process, the capacity each process consumes per unit of its activity times how hard that process is run, and set the unit's capacity use equal to that total.

constraints.name: cfq:
constraints.intent: For every final product, the amount sold must equal the amount actually made. For a product that is made by quality blending, the amount made is its blended quantity on a weight basis. For a product that is made by recipe blending, the amount made is the total of all its recipe blends, accumulating each component stream that the recipes draw in across all recipes.

constraints.name: specs[cfq,
constraints.intent: Each quality-blended product must respect every quality limit that actually applies to it, at both its lower and its upper limits. For a product and a specification, take the quality level that each eligible blending stream contributes, weighted by how much of that stream goes into the product measured on the basis that specification is expressed on, and add them up across all the eligible streams. This pooled quality must match the product's allowed limit applied to the product quantity on that same measure basis, with an over-or-under slack absorbing the difference so the blend can sit on the allowed side of the limit. Only impose this for product and specification combinations that carry a real limit, and skip the ones where no limit applies.

constraints.name: cfm:
constraints.intent: For each quality-blended product and each measure basis the model tracks for it, the product's quantity on that basis must equal the sum of its eligible blending streams converted to that same basis. Only impose this for the product and measure combinations that are required, and skip the rest.

constraints.name: revenue
constraints.intent: Total revenue is the selling price of each final product times the amount of it sold, added up over all final products. Set the revenue variable equal to that total.

constraints.name: recurrent
constraints.intent: Total recurrent operating cost has two pieces. The first is the operating cost of every unit times the capacity used at that unit, summed over all units. The second is the lead-related cost charged on the volume of motor gas produced. Set the operating cost variable equal to the sum of these two pieces.

constraints.name: purchase
constraints.intent: Total purchase cost combines what is paid for crude and what is paid for imports. For crude, multiply each crude's plant-gate price by the amount of that crude bought and sum over all crudes. For imports, multiply each imported component's price by the amount imported and sum over all imported components. Set the purchase cost variable equal to the sum of these two parts.

constraints.name: transport
constraints.intent: Total transport cost is the per-ton transport cost of each crude times the amount of that crude bought, summed over all crudes. Set the transport cost variable equal to that total.

constraints.name: profit
constraints.intent: Operating profit is the revenue left after paying the costs. Take total revenue and subtract the recurrent operating cost, the purchase cost, and the transport cost. Set the profit variable equal to that result.

query: Logistics is reworking the jetty schedule and there's a proposal on the table to hold total crude throughput to 240 thousand tons a cycle so the tanker berths don't get oversubscribed. If we run under that throughput ceiling, what does the optimizer return for profit?
expected_pyomo: model.crude_throughput_240 = Constraint(expr = sum(model.u[c] for c in model.cr) <= 240.0)

query: We're evaluating a supply-chain resilience target that would lean less on the bought-in fuel-oil feedstock. Suppose imported component intake is held to no more than 5 thousand tons per cycle — where does the plan and the margin land?
expected_pyomo: model.import_ceiling_5 = Constraint(expr = sum(model.import_var[c] for c in model.ci) <= 5.0)

query: We're leaning hard on Arabian crudes when Brega is right there in the supply book. Couldn't we require that Brega cover at least 25% of total crude purchased on a given cycle? What's the model seeing that's pushing us away from that supply-diversity hedge?
expected_pyomo: model.brega_floor_25 = Constraint(expr = model.u['brega'] >= 0.25 * sum(model.u[c] for c in model.cr))

model_narrative: This is a refinery planning problem. We buy crude oils and may import some components, run the refinery units at chosen process activity levels to distill and crack the crude into intermediate streams, and blend those streams into finished products that are then sold to meet demand. We decide how much of each crude to buy, how much of each component to import, how hard to run each process, how to move material through the transfer options, how to pool component streams into the quality-blended products, and which recipe blends to run for the recipe-blended products. The objective is to make the operating profit as large as possible.

sets.name: c
sets.doc: all commodities that move through the refinery, including crude oils, intermediate streams produced by the units, components that may be imported, and the final products

sets.name: cr
sets.doc: the crude oils that can be purchased and fed to the refinery

sets.name: ci
sets.doc: the commodities that can be bought in from outside as imported components

sets.name: cf
sets.doc: the final products that are sold

sets.name: cfq
sets.doc: the final products that are made by quality blending, where individual component streams are pooled and the blend must meet quality specifications

sets.name: cfr
sets.doc: the final products that are made by recipe blending, where fixed recipes set the proportions of component streams

sets.name: k
sets.doc: the productive process units of the refinery

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the processes the units can run, each consuming some commodities and producing others

sets.name: kuse
sets.doc: the pairs of a unit and a process that say which process runs on which unit

sets.name: bposs
sets.doc: the allowed pairs of a quality-blended final product and a component stream that may go into it; the first entry is the product and the second is the eligible blending stream

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the quality specifications a product can be measured against, such as vapor pressure, octane number, percent boiled at 212F, sulfur, and viscosity blending number

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the two ways a product quantity is measured, by volume or by weight

sets.name: ms
sets.doc: the pairs telling which measure basis each specification is expressed on; the first entry is the measure and the second is the specification. Volume-based specifications blend on a volume basis and weight-based specifications blend on a weight basis

sets.name: cfm
sets.doc: the pairs of a final product and a measure that the model is required to track for that product; the first entry is the product and the second is the measure

sets.name: l
sets.doc: the two kinds of quality limit, a lower limit and an upper limit

sets.name: r
sets.doc: the alternative blending recipes available for the recipe-blended products

sets.name: tr
sets.doc: the transfer options that move material between streams

params.name: m3tob
params.doc: barrels per cubic meter

params.name: pbmg
params.doc: lead content allowed in motor gas, in grams per liter

params.name: ppb
params.doc: price of lead, in dollars per kilogram

params.name: ocpb
params.doc: lead-related operating cost, in dollars per cubic meter of gasoline

params.name: crdat
params.doc: crude oil data table keyed by crude and attribute; attributes include supply (availability), price, transport (transport cost per ton), and gravity

params.name: ddat
params.doc: final product data table keyed by product and attribute; attributes include price (selling price per ton) and demand

params.name: kdat
params.doc: unit capacity data table keyed by unit and attribute

params.name: ap
params.doc: process yield of a commodity by a process, in 1000 tons of commodity per unit of process activity; a negative value means the commodity is consumed by the process and a positive value means it is produced

params.name: recipes
params.doc: recipe blending coefficient giving how much of a component stream goes into a recipe-blended product under a given recipe

params.name: at
params.doc: transfer coefficient of a commodity in a transfer option; a negative value means the commodity is consumed by the transfer and a positive value means it is produced

params.name: specs
params.doc: the quality limit value for a product at a lower or upper limit on a specification; a value of zero means that limit does not apply to that product and specification

params.name: prop
params.doc: physical property of a commodity for a given specification, such as its octane or sulfur level

params.name: char
params.doc: characterization factor converting a quantity of a commodity into a given measure basis; weight is one and volume reflects the commodity density

params.name: bp
params.doc: capacity utilization of a unit by a process, in cubic meters of capacity used per ton of process activity

params.name: kp
params.doc: available capacity of a unit, in 1000 cubic meters

params.name: oc
params.doc: operating cost of a unit, in dollars per cubic meter of capacity used

params.name: pcr
params.doc: plant-gate price of a crude oil, in dollars per ton

params.name: pimp
params.doc: price of an imported component, in dollars per ton

params.name: invent
params.doc: net inventory change of a commodity, in 1000 tons

params.name: dir
params.doc: the sign convention for a limit, minus one for a lower limit and plus one for an upper limit, used so the over-or-under slack pushes the blend in the right direction

vars.name: u
vars.doc: amount of crude purchased, in 1000 tons

vars.name: z
vars.doc: activity level of each process, in 1000 tons

vars.name: cap
vars.doc: capacity used at each unit, in 1000 cubic meters

vars.name: trans
vars.doc: level of each transfer activity, in 1000 tons

vars.name: import_var
vars.doc: amount of each component imported, in 1000 tons

vars.name: bq
vars.doc: amount of a component stream sent into a quality-blended product, in 1000 tons

vars.name: rb
vars.doc: level of a recipe blend run for a product, in 1000 tons

vars.name: q
vars.doc: quantity of a final product expressed on a given measure basis, in 1000 tons or 1000 cubic meters

vars.name: ov
vars.doc: amount by which a blend is over or under a quality limit, in 1000 units

vars.name: sales
vars.doc: amount of a final product sold, in 1000 tons; fixed to product demand

vars.name: revenue
vars.doc: total revenue from selling final products, in 1000 dollars

vars.name: recurrent
vars.doc: total recurrent operating cost, in 1000 dollars

vars.name: purchase
vars.doc: total cost of purchasing crude and imported components, in 1000 dollars

vars.name: transport
vars.doc: total transport cost of crude, in 1000 dollars

vars.name: profit
vars.doc: operating profit, in 1000 dollars

constraints.name: u[c]
constraints.intent: For every commodity, the refinery must stay in material balance. Everything that makes the commodity available has to equal everything that uses it up. On the supply side a commodity becomes available when it is a purchased crude, when the processes produce it, when the transfer activities yield it, and when it is brought in as an imported component. On the using side it is consumed as it is pooled into the quality-blended products, as it is drawn down or built up by the planned inventory change, and as it is consumed by the recipe blends of the recipe-blended products. Balance these two sides exactly for each commodity, remembering that the process and transfer yields already carry their own sign so that consumption shows up as a negative contribution.

constraints.name: cap[k]
constraints.intent: For each process unit, the capacity it uses must equal the capacity drawn by all the processes that run on it. Add up, over every process, the capacity each process consumes per unit of its activity times how hard that process is run, and set the unit's capacity use equal to that total.

constraints.name: cfq:
constraints.intent: For every final product, the amount sold must equal the amount actually made. For a product that is made by quality blending, the amount made is its blended quantity on a weight basis. For a product that is made by recipe blending, the amount made is the total of all its recipe blends, accumulating each component stream that the recipes draw in across all recipes.

constraints.name: specs[cfq,
constraints.intent: Each quality-blended product must respect every quality limit that actually applies to it, at both its lower and its upper limits. For a product and a specification, take the quality level that each eligible blending stream contributes, weighted by how much of that stream goes into the product measured on the basis that specification is expressed on, and add them up across all the eligible streams. This pooled quality must match the product's allowed limit applied to the product quantity on that same measure basis, with an over-or-under slack absorbing the difference so the blend can sit on the allowed side of the limit. Only impose this for product and specification combinations that carry a real limit, and skip the ones where no limit applies.

constraints.name: cfm:
constraints.intent: For each quality-blended product and each measure basis the model tracks for it, the product's quantity on that basis must equal the sum of its eligible blending streams converted to that same basis. Only impose this for the product and measure combinations that are required, and skip the rest.

constraints.name: revenue
constraints.intent: Total revenue is the selling price of each final product times the amount of it sold, added up over all final products. Set the revenue variable equal to that total.

constraints.name: recurrent
constraints.intent: Total recurrent operating cost has two pieces. The first is the operating cost of every unit times the capacity used at that unit, summed over all units. The second is the lead-related cost charged on the volume of motor gas produced. Set the operating cost variable equal to the sum of these two pieces.

constraints.name: purchase
constraints.intent: Total purchase cost combines what is paid for crude and what is paid for imports. For crude, multiply each crude's plant-gate price by the amount of that crude bought and sum over all crudes. For imports, multiply each imported component's price by the amount imported and sum over all imported components. Set the purchase cost variable equal to the sum of these two parts.

constraints.name: transport
constraints.intent: Total transport cost is the per-ton transport cost of each crude times the amount of that crude bought, summed over all crudes. Set the transport cost variable equal to that total.

constraints.name: profit
constraints.intent: Operating profit is the revenue left after paying the costs. Take total revenue and subtract the recurrent operating cost, the purchase cost, and the transport cost. Set the profit variable equal to that result.

query: It bugs me how much the whole plan hinges on Arabian Light — that's a lot of eggs in one basket if that grade ever gets squeezed. Shouldn't we hold Arabian Light to no more than 30% of total crude purchased so we're not so single-sourced? What's the model weighing that keeps leaning on it?
expected_pyomo: model.arabl_cap_30 = Constraint(expr = model.u['arabian-l'] <= 0.30 * sum(model.u[c] for c in model.cr))

query: Arabian Heavy is the cheapest barrel in the book and we've got plenty of allocation on it. Couldn't we make it the backbone of the slate and require it to be at least 60% of total crude purchased? What's the model trading off that stops it from loading up on the cheap grade?
expected_pyomo: model.arabh_floor_60 = Constraint(expr = model.u['arabian-h'] >= 0.60 * sum(model.u[c] for c in model.cr))
